nativeRange.habitat: Clear seaward reefs and drop-offs, typically 2–50 m depth

diet.notes: Has been documented grazing on algae from reef surfaces, including algae growing on sponges. Generally advised to offer a varied diet of marine-based seaweed, dried nori, spirulina-enriched foods, and macroalgae at least 3 times per week. Will also accept mysis and other meaty foods, but a predominantly plant-based diet tends to support immune health and reduce aggression.

summary: A large, active surgeonfish from Western Pacific reefs, generally considered reef-safe and valued for algae control. Best suited to very large, well-established aquariums; tends to be semi-aggressive toward similar-shaped species.
careNotes: Fowleri Tang (Acanthurus fowleri) is a large surgeonfish reaching around 45–50 cm, requiring a very large aquarium — generally advised to be at least 1500 L (approximately 400 US gallons) to provide adequate swimming room and territory. This species has been described as a 'solitary and wary' fish in the wild, generally grazing algae from reef surfaces. In captivity it tends to be semi-aggressive toward other surgeonfish and similar-shaped tankmates, while remaining largely peaceful toward unrelated species. Sources consistently recommend keeping only one individual per tank unless a very large system is available. A well-established aquarium with abundant live rock is generally advised to provide algae for natural grazing and territory within the rockwork. Diet should be heavily plant-based (nori, macroalgae, spirulina foods) with meaty foods as supplemental. Juveniles display distinct coloration from adults — juveniles are sometimes not recognized as the same species in the trade. As with all Acanthuridae, this species possesses a sharp, blade-like caudal-peduncle spine ('scalpel') that can inflict painful wounds during handling or net use; caution is recommended. Highly oxygenated water with moderate to high flow has been reported to be beneficial. Care level disagreement among sources: two sources rate this species as moderate/intermediate difficulty; one source rates it advanced, citing the need for a mature, large system. Intermediate has been selected as the consensus. Adult size disagreement: FishBase gives 45 cm SL (standard length); several retail sources state up to 50 cm TL (total length); range of 20–50 cm is used to capture both typical adult and maximum total length. Tank size disagreement: LiveAquaria and SaltwaterFish.com specify 360 US gallons (approximately 1363 L); ReefApp specifies 1500 L. Given this species' active swimming behaviour and large adult size, the larger value (1500 L) has been selected.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented in the hobbyist literature. No dedicated captive-breeding program appears to exist for this species at time of research.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species-specific page found at direct product URL; the collection page supplied by the manifest did not list this species individually.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (45 cm SL), temperature range (24–28°C), native range, taxonomy, and habitat. FishBase size is standard length vs. retail total-length values of 50 cm — both recorded in adultSizeCm range.

additional.name: AquaInfo
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm), temperature (24–28°C), salinity (1.025), native range, reef safety, and care notes. Care level rated advanced on this source; averaged with other sources to intermediate.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (45 cm), minimum tank volume (1500 L), salinity (~1.026), reef safety, and compatibility. Tank size of 1500 L adopted as the cautious round-number recommendation.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm), temperature (23–28°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), lifespan (10–15 years), and care notes.

fish.breedingNotes: No successful captive breeding documented in hobbyist literature.
